front: What is the statement of the Schröder-Bernstein theorem?
back: If there exist injective functions f: A → B and g: B → A between sets A and B, then there exists a bijective function h: A → B, establishing that A and B have the same cardinality, |A| = |B|.

front: What is the key intuitive idea behind the proof of the Schröder-Bernstein theorem?
back: The proof involves partitioning set A into two subsets, A_∞ and its complement, such that a bijection can be constructed by using f on one subset and the inverse of g on the other. This partition is found by examining the preimages of the sets under the compositions of f and g.

front: Why is the Schröder-Bernstein theorem important in the theory of sets and cardinality?
back: It provides a powerful method to prove that two sets are equinumerous (have the same cardinality) without explicitly constructing a bijection. It is sufficient to show that each set can be injected into the other.

front: In the context of the theorem, what does |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |A| imply?
back: According to the Schröder-Bernstein theorem, these two inequalities together imply that |A| = |B|. This is the cardinal number analogy to the property of antisymmetry in order relations.

front: What are the other common names for the Schröder-Bernstein theorem?
back: It is also known as the Cantor-Bernstein theorem or the Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein theorem, reflecting the independent contributions of Ernst Schröder and Felix Bernstein (based on work by Georg Cantor).
